rule: Secret know-how is protectable as property. Employees who gain knowledge under a duty of confidence cannot lawfully disclose or profit from it. Equity protects the relationship of trust.
holding:
GRAY, J.

It is the policy of the law, for the advantage of the public, to encourage and protect invention and commercial enterprise. If a man establishes a business and makes it valuable by his skill and attention, the good will of that business is recognized by the law as property. If he adopts and publicly uses a trade mark, he has a remedy, either at law or in equity, against those who undertake to use it without his permission. If he makes a new and useful invention of any machine or compos
bluebook: Peabody v. Norfolk, 98 Mass. 452, 98 Mass. 452 (1868) (Supreme Judicial Court of Massachusetts, 1868)

rule: A trade secret sold with a business is legally transferable. The seller's obligation to maintain secrecy survives the transaction.
holding:
PUTNAM J. delivered the opinion of the Court.

**3 The defendant contends that he has performed or offered to perform all that was required of him in the condition of the bond; while the plaintiff, on the other hand, denies any such performance or offer; and the question is to be settled by determining what was the true intent and meaning of the bond.
It is very clear that both parties had reference to the defendant’s exclusive and secret art of making chocolate, and that art was to be conveyed
bluebook: Vickery v. Welch, 19 Pick. 523, 36 Mass. 523 (Supreme Judicial Court of Massachusetts, 1837)

caseName: Board of Trade v. Christie Grain & Stock Co.
rule: Market quotations distributed under controlled conditions retain trade secret status. The key is controlled distribution — sharing information does not destroy secrecy when access is limited and governed by agreement.
holding:
ON WRIT of Certiorari to the United States Circuit Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit to review a decree which reversed a decree of the Circuit Court for the Western District of Missouri, enjoining the use and distribution of market quotations collected by the Chicago board of trade, and remanded the cause with instructions to dismiss the bill. Reversed. Also

ON WRIT of Certiorari to the United States Circuit Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit to review a decree which reversed a decr
bluebook: Board of Trade of City of Chicago v. Christie Grain & Stock Co., 198 U.S. 236, 25 S.Ct. 637 (Supreme Court of the United States, 1905)

caseName: E.I. du Pont de Nemours Powder Co. v. Masland
rule: Trade secret protection rests on the duty of good faith in confidential relationships, not on property rights in the information itself. The starting point is the confidential relationship, not whether the information qualifies as 'property.'
holding:
ON WRIT of Certiorari to the United States Circuit Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit to review a decree which reversed a decree of the District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania, enjoining defendant in a suit to prevent the use or disclosure of trade secrets, from disclosing such secrets to experts or witnesses produced during the taking of proof. Reversed and remanded for further proceedings.

See same case below, 140 C. C. A. 229, 224 Fed. 689.

The facts are stated in the op
bluebook: E. I. Du Pont De Nemours Powder Co. v. Masland, 244 U.S. 100, 37 S.Ct. 575 (Supreme Court of the United States, 1917)

caseName: Kewanee Oil Co. v. Bicron Corp.
rule: State trade secret law is not preempted by federal patent law. The two systems serve complementary purposes: trade secret protection encourages innovation without conflicting with the patent system's disclosure goals.
holding: Suit was brought to enjoin former employees of an unincorporated division of plaintiff from disclosing or using certain claimed trade secrets. The United States District Court for the Northern District of Ohio granted permanent injunction as to certain of the claimed trade secrets, and appeal and cross appeal were taken. The United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit reversed and remanded for dismissal, 478 F.2d 1074, and certiorari was granted. The Supreme Court, Mr. Chief Justice Bur
bluebook: Kewanee Oil Co. v. Bicron Corp., 416 U.S. 470, 94 S.Ct. 1879 (Supreme Court of the United States, 1974)

caseName: Coca-Cola Co. v. Coca-Cola Bottling Co.
rule: Coca-Cola's formula qualified as a trade secret — independent economic value from secrecy, access restricted to only two employees at a time, written formula stored in a bank vault. The paradigm example of reasonable efforts.

caseName: Follow Me Helicopter v. Shenzhen Ruihe
rule: Allegations of proprietary drone design data, restricted access, and nondisclosure agreements were sufficient to state a trade secret claim under the DTSA.
Systematic theft of proprietary radio technology source code violated the DTSA. Court awarded $764.6 million including punitive damages and attorneys' fees.
Giving numerous licensees access to a software database without consistent restrictions undermined the trade secret claim. Reasonable efforts require consistent security measures.

caseName: Bonito Boats v. Thunder Craft Boats
rule: Reverse engineering is a permissible means of discovery. Products sold on the open market without contractual restrictions cannot be shielded from analysis.
holding: Boat manufacturer brought suit to enjoin duplication of its unpatented boat hulls using “direct molding process,” allegedly in violation of Florida statute. The Circuit Court, Orange County, Cecil H. Brown, J., dismissed complaint with prejudice and boat manufacturer appealed. The District Court of Appeal, 487 So.2d 395, affirmed, and appeal was taken. The Florida Supreme Court, 515 So.2d 220, affirmed, and certiorari was granted. The Supreme Court, Justice O’Connor, held that Florida statute pr
bluebook: Bonito Boats, Inc. v. Thunder Craft Boats, Inc., 489 U.S. 141, 109 S.Ct. 971 (Supreme Court of the United States, 1989)

caseName: E.I. du Pont de Nemours & Co. v. Christopher
rule: Aerial surveillance of a plant under construction to reveal a proprietary chemical process constitutes improper means. Trade secret law requires companies to guard only against reasonable commercial norms violations, not all possible intrusions.
holding: Plaintiff brought suit alleging that defendants had wrongfully obtained photographs revealing plaintiff’s trade secrets which they then sold to undisclosed third party and plaintiff asked for damages and temporary and permanent injunctions. The United States District Court for the Eastern District of Texas, Joe J. Fisher, Chief Judge, denied defendants’ motion to dismiss for failure to state claim, and defendants took interlocutory appeal. In a case of first impression, the Court of Appeals, Gol
bluebook: E. I. duPont deNemours & Co. v. Christopher, 431 F.2d 1012, 431 F.2d 1012 (5th Cir. 1970) (United States Court of Appeals, Fifth Circuit, 1970)

rule: Using confidential manufacturing process information from prior employment, subject to ongoing confidentiality obligations, constitutes misappropriation — even without taking physical documents. The court awarded unjust enrichment damages.
holding: Manufacturer brought action against former employees, and corporation they formed, for misappropriation of trade secrets. The United States District Court for the Western District of Wisconsin, John C. Shabaz, J., granted judgment for manufacturer. Employees appealed. The Court of Appeals, Flaum, C.J., held that: (1) evidence was sufficient to support jury verdict of misappropriation of manufacturer’s process manuals; (2) manufacturer’s business conduct manuals were admissible; (3) manufacturer
bluebook: 3M v. Pribyl, 259 F.3d 587, 259 F.3d 587 (7th Cir. 2001) (United States Court of Appeals, Seventh Circuit, 2001)

rule: Having an NDA is not enough when the owner's actual conduct does not reinforce secrecy. If a company treats information casually despite contractual protections, courts may find reasonable efforts lacking. Disclosers must practice the protections their NDAs require.
Independent discovery through lawful means is a complete defense to trade secret misappropriation. If a competitor arrives at the same information through its own research, no violation has occurred.

A senior executive who accepted a competitor position while continuing to access confidential product development files posed a threat justifying injunctive relief. The new employer had reason to know the information was obtained improperly. Courts can enjoin threatened misappropriation and grant permanent injunctions to prevent ongoing use.
holding:
Background: Employer filed diversity action against former senior executive to protect its trade secrets. The United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania, R. Barclay Surrick, J., 2010 WL 571774, entered preliminary injunction enjoining defendant from working for competitor. Defendant filed interlocutory appeal.

Holdings: The Court of Appeals, Greenberg, Circuit Judge, held that:

[1] district court had discretion to enter preliminary injunction to enjoin former executi
bluebook: Bimbo Bakeries USA, Inc. v. Botticella, 613 F.3d 102, 613 F.3d 102 (3d Cir. 2010) (United States Court of Appeals, Third Circuit, 2010)

rule: Permanent injunction granted to prevent ongoing use of misappropriated trade secrets.

A solo inventor can satisfy reasonable secrecy efforts through detailed documentation, sharing only under confidentiality, and keeping the concept out of the public sphere. The court awarded damages based on lost licensing value. Absence of return/destruction obligations complicated damages but did not bar the claim.
holding:
Background: Competitor brought action against patentee for trade secret misappropriation, breach of contract, and to be named as inventor on patents for intervertebral spacers or implants. Jury returned verdict in favor of competitor on claim for trade secret misappropriation and awarded $4,295,760 in damages, but issue of inventorship was left for court. Each party submitted additional evidence on inventorship and objected to admissibility of opponent’s evidence.

Holdings: The District Court,
bluebook: Bianco v. Globus Medical, Inc., 30 F.Supp.3d 565, 30 F. Supp. 3d 565 (E.D. Tex. 2014) (United States District Court, E.D. Texas, Marshall Division, 2014)

rule: Technical ideas disclosed in confidence need not be reduced to physical form to be protected. Filing patent applications covering concepts received under a confidentiality agreement constitutes misappropriation. Trade secret status depends on secrecy, value, and control — not on the stage of development.
Punitive damages available for willful trade secret misappropriation.
Production injunction granted — court can require cessation of manufacturing using misappropriated process and limit the 'head start' advantage gained.
Court ordered destruction of code derived from misappropriated trade secrets.
Prompt action to enforce trade secret rights strengthens the plaintiff's case. Delay undermines credibility.

caseName: Data General v. Digital Computer Controls
rule: A culture of secrecy — where the organization consistently treats information as confidential through labeling, access controls, and employee awareness — supports a finding that reasonable efforts were taken.
Lax maintenance of secrecy protocols dooms a trade secret claim. If protections erode over time and the company does nothing to restore them, courts treat the information as abandoned.
Proprietary LiDAR designs and simulation tools qualified as trade secrets — significant R&D investment, strict access controls, measurable competitive advantage.
Software algorithms can qualify as trade secrets when they represent proprietary methods not generally known in the industry.
A proprietary pricing algorithm qualified as a trade secret. Operational methods and analytical frameworks not known outside the business can qualify for protection.

Reverse engineering renders information 'readily ascertainable.' If a product can be lawfully acquired and analyzed, the underlying design is not protectable as a trade secret absent a contractual restriction.
High economic value bolsters trade secret status. The more a secret contributes to competitive advantage, the more courts credit the claim.
Over-claiming yet under-documenting 'secret' processes undermines trade secret status. Companies must be precise about what they protect.

caseName: Silvaco Data Systems v. Intel Corp.
rule: A well-drafted NDA demonstrates intent and commitment to protection and is a relevant factor in the reasonable efforts analysis.

caseName: Tewari De-Ox Systems v. Mountain States/Rosen
rule: An overbroad NDA can undermine trade secret claims by failing to define the protected information with precision.
Overly broad invention assignment clauses are unenforceable when they fail to distinguish between inventions using employer resources and those developed independently.
California noncompetes are void under Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600. Strong public policy favors open competition and employee mobility. The 'narrow restraint' exception was rejected.

caseName: Provision Living v. Provision Senior Living
rule: A narrowly tailored non-solicitation provision tied to trade secret protection was enforced — protecting confidential customer relationships and operational strategies.
A disgruntled employee who transferred confidential manufacturing data to outside parties and the media posed a plausible internal threat justifying trade secret claims.
Systematic use of access controls, password protections, and information segmentation supported the claim that information was treated as a trade secret.
Enforceable restrictive covenants, when paired with access controls and employee training, contribute to the reasonable efforts analysis.

Inevitable disclosure doctrine: when a former executive joins a direct competitor in a competing role, injunctive relief is appropriate because the position would almost certainly lead to use of trade secrets — even without proof of actual disclosure. The court granted both preliminary and permanent injunctive relief.
holding: Soft drink manufacturer brought action against competitor and former managerial employee, seeking to prevent employee from divulging manufacturer’s trade secrets and confidential information in his new job with competitor and from assuming any duties with competitor relating to beverage pricing, marketing, and distribution. The United States District Court for the Northern District of Illinois, George W. Lindberg, J., granted injunction, and appeal was taken. The Court of Appeals, Flaum, Circuit
bluebook: PepsiCo, Inc. v. Redmond, 54 F.3d 1262, 54 F.3d 1262 (7th Cir. 1995) (United States Court of Appeals, Seventh Circuit, 1995)

rule: Disclosing proprietary programming concepts and marketing strategies during joint venture negotiations without an NDA means the information is not protected. Even highly sensitive commercial information loses trade secret status when shared without formal controls.
holding: Promoters of cable program brought action against broadcaster after investment negotiation fell through, alleging misappropriation of trade secrets, breach of contract, and related torts. On broadcaster’s motion for summary judgment, the District Court, Heyburn, J., held that: (1) unfair competition and quasi-contract claims were preempted by Kentucky Uniform Trade Secrets Act (KUTSA); (2) no express or implied contract existed; (3) broadcaster was not promisorily estopped from competing with pr
bluebook: Auto Channel, Inc. v. Speedvision Network, LLC, 144 F.Supp.2d 784, 144 F. Supp. 2d 784 (W.D. Ky. 2001) (United States District Court, W.D. Kentucky, Louisville Division, 2001)

rule: Digital controls — login protections, document labeling, and defined user roles — strengthen trade secret rights. Platform architecture can make or break trade secret status in digital environments.
bluebook: 7EDU Impact Acad. Inc. v. Ya You, 760 F. Supp. 3d 981, 550 U.S. 544, 555 (Federal, 2026)

rule: After a pilot project for AI fuel optimization tools ended, launching a similar product using information shared in confidence and subject to use restrictions states a plausible misappropriation claim. Courts enforce use limitations even after collaboration ends.
bluebook: C3.Ai Inc. v. Cummins, Inc., 8 In 2019, 11 C3 provides a 12 (Federal, 2026)

rule: Reverse engineering is permissible unless an enforceable agreement restricts it. When a product is lawfully obtained and no contract prohibits analysis, the underlying design is not shielded by trade secret law.
holding:
Background: Assignee filed action against competitor alleging infringement of patent that disclosed wire tier device that was used to bale recyclables or solid waste for easier handling. The United States District Court for the Southern District of Texas, Lynn N. Hughes, J., granted summary judgment of noninfringement to competitor. Assignee appealed.

Holdings: The Court of Appeals, Prost, Circuit Judge, held that:

[1] elongated operator body could be operably coupled to one or more operator e
bluebook: Accent Packaging, Inc. v. Leggett & Platt, Inc., 707 F.3d 1318, 707 F.3d 1318 (Fed. Cir. 2013) (United States Court of Appeals, Federal Circuit, 2013)

rule: Having confidentiality terms in a reseller relationship is insufficient without consistent technical safeguards. Absence of access controls and monitoring made it harder to show reasonable steps were taken.

Removing confidential airline revenue data from the company's control can be actionable as improper disclosure. But no signed NDAs, no documented access restrictions, and no internal protocols means the reasonable efforts requirement fails. The court granted summary judgment for the defendant because the plaintiff had not demonstrated that its data qualified as a trade secret.
holding: Background: Former employer whose primary product was an auditing software that analyzed ticket fares for airlines and travel agencies to ensure tickets sold for the appropriate price brought action against former employee, a developer and licenser of revenue accounting software, alleging claims including breach of employment agreement, and misappropriation of trade secrets under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA). Following a bench trial, the United States District Court for the Dis
bluebook: AirFacts, Inc. v. de Amezaga, 909 F.3d 84, 909 F.3d 84 (4th Cir. 2018) (United States Court of Appeals, Fourth Circuit, 2018)

rule: A 'claims handling process' described only in general terms lacks the specificity needed to qualify as a trade secret. Business acumen or professional judgment is not protectable unless tied to concrete, identifiable information. Vague allegations result in early dismissal.

Once content is published to the internet and circulated publicly, it no longer satisfies the secrecy requirement. Public disclosure destroys trade secret status. The court declined to impose liability for use of information already in the public domain.
holding:
Religious organization brought action against newspaper and reporters, alleging copyright infringement arising from newspaper article which included statements from religious works. On defendants’ motion for summary judgment, the District Court, Brinkema, J., held that: (1) fair use defense was not barred by doctrine of unclean hands; (2) publication of article was “fair use”; (3) newspaper was entitled to attorney’s fees; and (4) newspaper did not misappropriate organization’s trade secrets.

G
bluebook: Religious Technology Center v. Lerma, 908 F.Supp. 1362, 908 F. Supp. 1362 (E.D. Va. 1995) (United States District Court, E.D. Virginia, Alexandria Division, 1995)

rule: Inconsistent application of secrecy policies — sharing some materials widely while protecting others — undermines trade secret claims. Reasonable efforts must be clear and uniform.
A complaint that fails to describe how information was protected or how the defendant's conduct was improper — amounting to a complaint about competition, not misappropriation — will be dismissed.
bluebook: American Registry, LLC v. Hanaw, 550 U.S. 544, 555, 127 S.Ct. 1955, 167 (United States District Court, M.D. Florida, Fort Myers Division, 2013)

rule: In the Bratz dolls dispute, the court awarded a reasonable royalty reflecting the licensing value of trade secrets used to develop a competing product line. Expert testimony established value in context of the doll market.
holding: Background: Toy manufacturer brought action against former employee for breach of employment agreement after employee disclosed his idea for a line of fashion dolls to a competitor, and against competitor for copyright infringement. The United States District Court for the Central District of California, Stephen G. Larson, J., 2008 WL 5598275, awarded manufacturer $10 million in damages, imposed constructive trust transferring competitor’s “Bratz” trademark portfolio to manufacturer, and enjoine
bluebook: Mattel, Inc. v. MGA Entertainment, Inc., 616 F.3d 904, 616 F.3d 904 (9th Cir. 2010) (United States Court of Appeals, Ninth Circuit, 2010)

rule: Denied ex parte seizure where urgency and imminent risk were not demonstrated. Reinforced the narrow application of DTSA seizure — available only when defendant's actions pose an immediate and irreparable threat. Delay in seeking relief also weakened the case for immediate injunctive relief.

Court awarded both attorneys' fees and enhanced damages for willful misappropriation of proprietary mortgage underwriting software and bad-faith litigation tactics. The award aimed at punishing wrongful conduct and deterring similar behavior.

Former Goldman Sachs employee convicted under the Economic Espionage Act for stealing high-frequency trading code. Conviction overturned on appeal due to technical issues, but the case demonstrated the reach of criminal enforcement for trade secret theft.
bluebook: United States v. Mead Corp., 533 U.S. 218 (2001)

rule: Strategic venue selection can provide procedural advantages in IP litigation. The court emphasized that the chosen venue had proper jurisdiction and experience handling complex intellectual property claims.
System logs served as critical evidence of misappropriation under the Economic Espionage Act. Digital forensics can establish the timing, scope, and method of trade secret theft.
bluebook: United States v. Mead Corp., 533 U.S. 218 (2001)
